transcript.text: This is a pretty big deal. This recent
breakthrough by the team behind Kimmy,
which is like another DeepSeek,
completely shatters our limitation of
building better AI models. Not only does
this fix the amnesia problem of current
AI, but in fact, they've kind of created
a new architecture design which can even
reconfigure itself and kind of learn
things on the fly. Before we jump into
the paper, let's understand the problem
a bit more. Imagine you're taking the
hardest math exam of your life. Not a
quick quiz, but a brutal problem that
requires like 50 steps. Now, you start
off strong. You can easily do step one,
step two, step three, and so on. It's
pretty easy. But once you get to step
48, your brain is overloaded. It's
completely overwhelmed by the sheer
volume of numbers you had to process.
Your working memory just maxes out.
You're staring at these massive
complicated calculations, but you've
completely lost the thread of what you
originally set out to solve. It's like
you kind of have localized amnesia.
You're thinking so deeply that you're
just drowning out your own earlier
thoughts. This is a very common human
problem. Our working memory has strict
physical limits. If we think too long or
too deep, we just get overwhelmed. And
strangely enough, it turns out that the
massive trillion parameter AI models
that we use every day, like GPT or
Gemini, they're suffering from the exact
same amnesia. But this recent
breakthrough actually presents a cure
for this AI amnesia. So the paper is
called attention residuals by the Kimmy
team. And this is a super fascinating
read, but it's extremely technical. So
in this video, I'm going to break
everything down for you so it's easy to
understand. Let's jump right in. First,
let's start with a brief background on
the design of AI models today. To
understand why the AI gets overwhelmed,
we first need to look at how these
models are actually built. When we talk
about large language models, we're
talking about really deep models that
have over like a 100 sequential blocks
or layers. And each block performs a ton
of really complex matrix calculations.
Conceptually, you can think of it like
this. The first layer would process the
raw text. The next layer might look at
grammatical patterns. The next one
starts to understand intent. The next
layer might analyze text even deeper.
And the next one even deeper. By the
time you reach like the 120th layer, the
model is performing some incredibly
abstract and deep thinking. Now, for
years, we couldn't actually build these
models to be very deep. If you made the
model too deep, it would be really hard
to train. Really quickly, here's how an
AI model learns. During training, the AI
compares its response with the answer.
And if it gets the answer wrong, then it
incurs a loss or a penalty. And this
penalty flows backwards through the
model to tweak its parameters so that
next time it would be less wrong. This
is a process called back propagation and
gradient descent. However, if you build
these models too deeply, in other words,
if you stack too many layers together,
this learning signal, which flows
backwards through the model, would
simply vanish before it could even reach
the beginning of the model. So, this
problem is called the vanishing gradient
problem. Fortunately, in 2015,
researchers came up with a fix called
residual connections. The idea was
really simple. Instead of each layer
trying to completely transform the
information it receives, you let the
original data skip the layer entirely
and add it back to the end. Think of
this as like a shortcut or highway for
information to zip through the network
without much change. And it's this
highway that allowed us to scale models
from only a few dozen layers to hundreds
or even thousands of layers deep. And
this is how we design modern AI models.
But as the Kimi team points out in their
new paper, this highway is actually a
huge fundamental flaw. In fact, this is
what leads to the amnesia that AI
experiences when it handles a ton of
stuff at once. Because we're just adding
the results of every layer together,
layer after layer. The final result is
just one massive cumulative pile of
data. The total magnitude of the signal
increases the deeper it gets. As this
signal gets larger and larger, the
importance of any single layer's
contribution shrinks. So, this kind of
buries early information and makes it
really hard to retrieve or remember.
Essentially, we've built a system where
the AI is thinking so hard and adding so
many new thoughts to the pile that it
can no longer remember what it was
thinking at the beginning of the
problem. Here's a nice analogy on how
you can think of this. Imagine 50 chefs
lined up in a kitchen representing like
50 layers of our AI. The first chef
chops some delicate basil and throws it
in the empty pot. They pass it to chef
two, who dumps in a gallon of beef
broth. Chef three adds some potatoes.
Chef four adds tomatoes. Chef five hauls
over a whole crate of their own
ingredients. Chef six tips in a sack of
his ingredients. And Chef 7 dumps in
some other ingredients, and so on and so
forth until it reaches the 50th chef.
Now, because it has gone through 50
chefs, each pouring their own
ingredients in there, not only is the
bowl of soup just overflowing, but when
you taste the final soup, it gets really
hard to taste the original basil from
the first chef because everything is
just mixed into this giant slob. And
this also creates a second problem. The
layers in this deep AI model have to
produce stronger and stronger signals
just to have any noticeable influence on
the final result. It's like these layers
have to scream to be heard over the
noise of earlier layers. Going back to
our analogy, after the 50 chefs make
this massive pot of soup, if you decide
that it needs to be a bit saltier, well,
you can't just add a pinch of salt
anymore. You need to add a massive bag
of it just to make it a bit saltier
because of the sheer volume of the pot
of soup. At this point, you might be
wondering, well, if the problem is just
the signal getting too big, can't we
just scale it down? For example, can't
we add a volume dial to earlier layers
to turn down their signals and also
volume dials to later layers to amplify
their signals? Well, in short, we've
already tried that. There are already
some clever ways such as scaled residual
paths or multiream recurrences, but in
short, none of these actually solved the
core issue. They all had the same
fundamental flaw with each layer getting
the single aggregated results of all the
previous layers. Everything was just
mixed and compressed together like a
giant pot of soup with so many
ingredients you can't make out anything.
Now the proposal from this paper from
the Kimmy team is absolutely brilliant.
They took a step back and actually
realized that we already have a solution
to this. Now before I go over the
solution, it's important to give you a
brief history of AI language models. You
see, current AI models are based off of
this transformer architecture. And this
allows AI to do some incredible stuff
like writing a poem for you or writing
an essay or even a complete research
report. It's able to understand natural
language so well because the transformer
is able to understand context. For
example, take the sentence, the cat
didn't cross the street because it was
too tired. Note that it could refer to
the street or the cat, but when we
humans read it, we know that it's
referring to the cat because of context.
Now, before transformers came out, it
was actually really hard for language
models to actually understand this
context. Before transformers, the best
thing we had was recurrent neural
networks or RNN for short. The
architecture looks something like this.
And these models processed text one word
at a time. It basically compresses
everything that it sees before inside a
hidden state, which you can think of as
like a memory. Now, if you give an RNN a
huge amount of text to process, then it
suffers heavily from the same kind of
amnesia. By the time the model reached
the end of your paragraph, it would
easily forget earlier parts. That's
because it's compressing the past
information so much. As it reads the
paragraph, it keeps compressing and
compressing that eventually its memory
just becomes overloaded and it fails to
remember the earliest stuff. But
transformers on the other hand was able
to fix this issue by introducing this
attention mechanism. Instead of passing
along a single compressed memory, the
model can look back at any previous word
directly and see which ones are relevant
to the current word. That means it could
selectively get exactly the information
it needed. And this was the breakthrough
that made modern AI possible. In fact,
if you're curious about the technical
details on how a transformer actually
works, definitely see this video where I
did a deep dive on the transformer.
Anyways, the design of this transformer
led to the aha moment from the Kimmy
researchers. Modern transformer models
already solved this amnesia problem of
older recurrent neural networks by
processing text using attention. But
instead of the text dimension in a
paragraph, what if they applied the same
fix for the depth dimension of an AI
model? After all, an AI model that's
hundreds of thousands of layers deep is
pretty much the same thing as a
paragraph of text. And the way it's
currently designed is like a recurrent
neural network. As data flows forward,
it compresses all its calculations into
one cumulative signal layer after layer,
which makes it suffer from amnesia.
signals get diluted, intermediate steps
get entangled, and by the time you reach
the final layers, the original signal is
barely recoverable. Just like an RNN
fails to process a long piece of text.
So, it seems like we might have the
answer hiding in plain sight. What if we
added attention to these residual
connections of a very deep AI model?
Hence the name of the paper, attention
residuals. So, here's how the Kimmy team
designed it. It's a lot of math under
the hood, but let me explain this to you
in simple terms. Instead of just being a
conveyor belt where information flows
from one end to the other linearly, now
for every layer, it can actually reach
back and look at any previous layers
output. This prevents the AI from
getting confused or forgetting its
earlier thoughts as it thinks deeper.
Now, to do this, the model uses a system
of query key and value vectors or QKV
for short. exactly like the attention
mechanism of the transformer. Again, if
you're not familiar with the
transformer, I highly recommend you
watch this video first. But anyways, you
can think of each layer as having a
specific query or question. It's like
they're each asking, okay, what do I
need from all the previous layers before
me? What information am I looking for?
And then the key is like a label for
each previous layer describing its
contents. The key is like each layer
raising its hand and saying, "All right,
here's where I am. Here's what I
represent. See if your query matches
what I have to offer." And then the
value is the actual information inside
each layer. So, how it works is let's
say this is our current layer that's
processing data. Well, it could compare
its query to every layer's key before it
and see how well they match. And if it's
a good match, that means that previous
layer would have some relevant
information for the current layer. and
then it would mix in the value of that
layer with the output. This brilliant
design allows the AI to stay perfectly
focused on the most important details.
No matter how many steps of thinking it
has to do, no matter how many layers the
model has, each layer can selectively
choose which layers information to use
instead of just using an incredibly
messy cumulative signal. Going back to
our chef analogy now, instead of just
each chef throwing their ingredients
into a massive pot of soup and mixing
everything together, with this attention
residual method, it's like each layer
walks into a buffet. Every previous
layer lays out its output in a clean,
separate dish. One tray for potatoes,
one for carrots, one for tomatoes, and
so on. Each layer gets to selectively
choose exactly what it needs. Some
layers matter a lot, while others get
ignored entirely. And that's the beauty
of this design. Each layer can adjust
what it searches based on what it's
processing. If the model sees a word
like therefore, it might pull heavily
from the layers that specialize in logic
or order. Or when it sees the word
betrayed, it might shift its focus
toward layers that involve emotion or
relationships. At least conceptually,
that's how you can think of this. And in
theory, this should solve the amnesia
problem, right? Well, just when you
think we solved it, reality slaps you
hard in the face. You see, when we scale
this to the size of the top AI models
with hundreds of billions or even over a
trillion parameters, you run straight
into a brick wall of physics
limitations. If you've been keeping up
with AI lately, you've probably seen a

the real question. Can you actually turn
any of that into something useful?
That's the problem I have. These AI
clips are great, but I still need to

further myself. You still need editing

highquality videos. And ready to publish

>> Long ago, the story of Rome began with

After all, even the H100 GPU only has 80

So, how the AI labs overcome this is
currently the model lives inside
multiple racks in a data center. This is
also called pipeline parallelism where
they slice the model up. For example,
layers 1 to 10 might be on server rack
A. 11 to 20 might be on the next server
rack in a different part of the data
center, and they would each be connected
by fiber optic cables. Now,
traditionally, this ain't a problem.
When server A finishes its work, it's
just one single compressed output that
would be sent to the next data server.
And then once that block finishes, it
would send one output to the next server
and so on. It's just one linear
sequence. But with this new buffet style
of attention residuals where every layer
needs to inspect the previous layers to
make its decision, suddenly you aren't
sending one output in one direction
anymore. If for each layer it has to
talk to all the previous layers before
it, then the data traffic just explodes.
It requires a lot more communication
between different server acts which
makes it extremely inefficient. So with
this current attention residuals design,
you can't actually apply this to huge
models in a data center. We also need to
consider the infrastructure limitations.
So the Kimmy team came up with something
brilliant called block attention
residuals. In simple terms, the model is
basically broken down into separate
blocks. And within each block, we have
these new attention residuals which can
understand context. But between
different blocks, we just have the old
linear communication. The layers inside
each block combine their work into one
representative summary and only this is
sent to the next server rack. So this
way it includes the benefits of these
attention residuals where each layer can
look at previous layers outputs in a
buffet style, but it can also be
efficiently applied to multiple server
racks without having an insane amount of

everything down. So, it's awesome that
the Kimmy team didn't just publish
something theoretical. They even applied
it to real life infrastructure to make
sure it works. All right, so that's the
design of everything. But how well does

shocking. Here's one figure from the
paper for your reference. The blue line
is the base model without attention
residuals. The red line is with full

with block attention residuals where you
segment the model based on server racks.
And you can see that for both models
with attention residuals, they achieve
the same amount of performance during
training while using 1.25 times less

where a single training run can cost

means saving millions of dollars or

models for the exact same budget. In

outperforms DeepSseek's recent

for manifold constraint
hyperconnections. So, this is a huge
breakthrough, but efficiency isn't the
most exciting part. The real
breakthrough shows up in reasoning. In
GPQA diamond, which is like a really

attention residuals jumps by 7.5 points,
which is a huge leap. This shows that
the model actually performs better,
especially for multi-step reasoning
tasks. And if you look at MMLU, which is
another benchmark designed to evaluate
world knowledge and reasoning
capabilities. So this covers things from
STEM subjects, humanities, social
sciences, law, and more. Again, the
model with attention residuals performs
better. In terms of all these general
knowledge benchmarks, the model with
attention residuals just scores better
than the base model. The same pattern
applies if you look at specialized tasks
in math and coding. These are tasks that
require really long multi-step
reasoning, not just pattern matching.
And that's exactly where this new
architecture shines. Now, under the
hood, what's really happening is that
the model fixes the core issue we talked
about earlier, which is signal dilution.
Instead of letting internal
representations grow uncontrollably as
they pass through layer after layer, as
you can see from this red line from a
normal model, adding attention residuals
would keep the signal bounded and
stable. You can see this green line
looks a lot more stable. It doesn't just
explode exponentially, and the earlier
signal doesn't get buried. And because
of that, something subtle but incredibly
important happens. The learning process
itself becomes more balanced. So if you
look at this other figure from the

signals that tell the model how to
improve. And as you can see with
attention residuals, this signal is also
distributed more evenly across all
layers. Every part of the network gets a
clear usable learning signal. The entire
system becomes healthier. But we're not
done yet. This breakthrough also changes
how we design models. Traditionally,
going deeper was quite risky. Too many
layers and the signal would collapse.
Researchers were terrified of making
networks too deep because the amnesia
would take over. They were forced to
build wider models instead. But these
were slow and inefficient. But now with
attention residuals, that constraint
disappears. Now we can actually build
deeper and deeper models without this
amnesia problem. But this brings us to
the next question which is should we
build wider models or deeper models?
Which ones actually perform better?
Well, the Kimmy researchers also took
that into consideration and they ran a
massive experiment. They trained 25
different models with different shapes.
Some of them are short and wide, others
deep and narrow, but all of them had the
same number of parameters so that it's
an equal comparison. And the goal was to
see which models performed better. So
here is the result. This is figure seven
from the paper. It's quite a technical
figure, but the takeaway here is that
both architectures improved as they
became deeper, not wider. But the base
models without attention residuals hit a
wall. If you go too deep, the
performance collapsed. However, the
models with attention residuals pushed
this even further. They kept improving
and you can build even deeper models. In
other words, depth is no longer a
limitation. It's an advantage. And this
unlocks something powerful. Now you can
build models that think in longer
chains. Models that specialize layer by
layer. Models that don't lose their
earlier reasoning as they go deeper. In
fact, the most fascinating part is when
you actually take a look inside the
model. The researchers visualized the
attention patterns across different
layers, essentially mapping how each
layer pulls information from other
layers. And what they found almost looks
like a brain scan. First, there's
locality. So most layers still rely on
their immediate neighbors. That's the
default step-by-step processing. But
then you see something new. You see some
sudden long range connections. A layer
deep in the network would suddenly reach
all the way back to like the earlier
layers completely skipping everything in
between. It's like the model realizes,
wait, I need to revisit the original
premise and it dynamically rewires
itself to do exactly that. It creates
its own residual connections on the fly.
And then there's specialization.
Different types of layers behave
differently. Some act like short-term
memory, focusing locally. Others act
like global coordinators pulling
information from across the entire
network. The system isn't uniform
anymore. It's structured, organized,
almost hierarchical in function, exactly
like how the human brain works. And this
leads to a profound shift in how we
think about neural networks. With
attention residuals, they're no longer
static pipelines. They're actually
dynamic systems. Instead of a fixed
sequence of operations, the model can
now constantly reconfigure itself based
on what it receives. For every input it
receives, it builds a custom pathway
through its layers, uses it, and then
discards it. It's not just computing,
but it's like it's routing and selecting
and deciding. And this brings up a
really fascinating insight. By using
these attention residuals, they don't
just improve performance, but they
change the nature of the system itself.
This turns a static structure into
something fluid and adaptive. If you
zoom out, it starts to look strangely
familiar. It starts to resemble the
human brain. Because when humans think,
we don't just accumulate thoughts
blindly. We pause, we go back, and we
retrieve past information. We also
ignore irrelevant ideas and focus on
what matters. we kind of manage our own
internal attention and that's exactly
what these attention residuals do. And
also as we learn our brain is constantly
rewiring itself, strengthening useful
connections, pruning away the noise and
also building new connections that it
finds would be relevant. This ability is
known as neuroplasticity.
And now with these attention residuals,
AI can kind of do the same thing. It's
not just remembering better, but
deciding what to remember. It can rewire

Perhaps this might be the next step in
